Preparation and Pre-Lab Work (7 Marks) ● Pre-Lab Assignments: Assessment of pre-lab assignments or quizzes that test understanding of the upcoming experiment. ● Understanding of Theory: Evaluation based on students’ preparation and understanding of the theoretical background related to the experiments. 2. Conduct of Experiments (7 Marks) ● Procedure and Execution: Adherence to correct procedures
and following safety protocols. ● Skill Proficiency: Proficiency in handling equipment
and troubleshooting skills during the experiments. ● Teamwork: Collaboration and participation in group experiments. 3. Lab Reports and Record Keeping (6 Marks) ● Quality of Reports: Clarity
completeness and accuracy of lab reports. Proper documentation of experiments

data analysis and conclusions. ● Timely Submission: Adhering to deadlines for submitting lab reports/rough record and maintaining a well-organized fair record. 4. Viva Voce (5 Marks) ● Oral Examination: Ability to explain the experiment
results and underlying principles during a viva voce session. Final Marks Averaging: The final marks for preparation

and record are the average of all the specified experiments in the syllabus. Evaluation Pattern for End Semester Examination (50 Marks) 1. Procedure/Preliminary Work/Design/Algorithm (10 Marks) ● Procedure Understanding and Description: Clarity in explaining the procedure and understanding each step involved. ● Preliminary Work and Planning: Thoroughness in planning and organizing materials/equipment. ● Algorithm Development: Correctness and efficiency of the algorithm related to the experiment. ● Creativity and logic in algorithm or experimental design. 2. Conduct of Experiment/Execution of Work/Programming (15 Marks) ● Setup and Execution: Proper setup and accurate execution of the experiment or programming task. 3. Result with Valid Inference/Quality of Output (10 Marks) ● Accuracy of Results: Precision and correctness of the obtained results. ● Analysis and Interpretation: Validity of inferences drawn from the experiment or quality of program output. 4. Viva Voce (10 Marks) ● Ability to explain the experiment
procedure results and answer related questions ● Proficiency in answering questions related to theoretical and practical aspects of the subject. 5. Record (5 Marks) ● Completeness

and accuracy of the lab record submitted SEMESTER 7 POLYMER ENGINEERING SEMESTER: S7 FIBRE SCIENCE & TECHNOLOGY Course Code PEPOT 741 CIE Marks 40 Teaching Hours/Week (L: T:P: R) 3:0:0:0 ESE Marks 60 Credits 3 Exam Hours
Hrs. 30 Min. Prerequisites (if any) None Course Type Theory Course Objectives: 1. Develop knowledge on manufacturing
testing and applications of fibres 2. Impart knowledge on various kinds of fibres. SYLLABUS Module No. Syllabus Description Contact Hours 1 Introduction
conversion from one system to another
use of fibres in the rubber and plastic industry
present status and future prospects Fibres used in polymer industry-types and sources
concept of order in fibres and polymers
orientation and fibre properties 9 2 Methods of investigating fibre structure. use of animal and mineral fibres. Detailed study of fibre properties such as mechanical properties
moisture regain optical properties and fibre friction
relation between fibre properties and structure
uses of vegetable fibres such as cotton
physical structure of fibre forming polymers
properties and testing of viscose rayon
finishing and dyeing. 4 Yarn and textile production from fibres
brief idea about spinning of cotton yarns and blends .Yarn properties such as count appearance
dynamic mechanical properties and their importance
thermal properties 9 Course Assessment Method (CIE: 40 marks

ESE: 60 marks) Continuous Internal Evaluation Marks (CIE): Attendance Assignment/ Microproject Internal Examination-1 (Written) Internal Examination- 2 (Written) Total 5 15 10 10 40 End Semester Examination Marks (ESE) In Part A
all questions need to be answered and in Part B
each student can choose any one full question out of two questions Part A Part B Total

Prerequisites (if any) Nil Course Type Theory Course Objectives: 1. Understand the Foundations of CAD Systems. 2. Develop Skills in Geometric Modeling and Transformations. 3. Master Computerized Manufacturing and CNC Programming. SYLLABUS Module No. Syllabus Description Contact Hours 1 Introduction to CAD (Computer aided design): CAD system architecture- Packages for CAD/CAM/CAE/CAPP. Hardware in CAD components
data Exchange Formats Geometric transformations: Transformation of points and line
combined transformations. Algebraic and geometric forms
Bezier curves and B-spline curves. 9 2 Geometric Modeling: Plane surface
B-spline surfaces and their modelling techniques. 9 Solid models and representation scheme
coordinate systems for solid modelling. 3 Computerized Manufacture Planning and Control System: Computer Aided Process Planning
outputs and benefits. Flexible Manufacturing Systems: Fundamentals of Group Technology and Flexible Manufacturing Systems
AS/RS and Automatic parts identification systems and data capture. 9 4 Computer Numerical Control: Introduction
programming of simple components in turning
programming with canned cycles. Cutter radius compensations. Robot Technology: Robot anatomy
sensors in robotics. Robot programming methods: on-line and offline methods. Robot industrial applications: material handling
processing and assembly and inspection. 9 Course Assessment Method (CIE: 40 marks
